item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I think this could be interpreted as parodying how researchers try to study anomalies, and it's as if all the answers they are getting is "IT'S MAGIC!! WHY WON'T YOU JUST GET THAT?!"

And a frustrated researcher would write this out based on his work experience.

And that's what you're supposed to get out of it if you thought this was a J?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: right so its meant to be a -j that is actually real. that makes sense. however in-universe this is designed to trick people that it is a -j by joking about it, so that means that it has to be funny in universe to people that don't know about its gimmick. and its not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because it's either a supplement or a -J in its own right, but not both. As this was added to the -J list as a bona fide entry, we tagged it as a such. If its going to be a supplement, it needs to be removed from the list, and its parent page should be set.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why was the "supplement" tag removed, and all the other tags added? And why was it added to the joke list? This is fairly obviously meant to be a supplement to 2615, and just that.

Edit: I see that Fae, the author of this, added it to the joke page. This only furthers my confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the article for its reference to the possibility of J's being canon. I'm kinda on the line on if I should upvote or neutral though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how this was used, but no-voting for now, It just isn't funny enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since this is linked from the Joke page hub, I'm going to judge it as a joke article. It's not even remotely funny. I'm not even sure it's trying to be deliberately humorous. It's shallow and very juvenile. Downvoted.

item.title: Note which may be relevant
item.content:encoded:
Regardless of tags, this is present in the Joke SCP list. While this is ultimately a staff decision on what that entails, I will note that everything linked on the mainlist (which isn't a splash screen) is tagged as "scp", even if they are "tales in sheep clothing" (eg. SCP-2718, your own SCP-2998).

But then again, there is precedent in format screwing the mainlist, like Kate did a couple of times. Again, just mentioning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's not technically a -J, but the whole article hinges on this being just as real as any other -J, and therefore it should be subject to the exact same regulations, since the article implies that the other (funny) -Js are from the same source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, a joke article is a joke article. I'm going to consider it based on its merits as a joke, in-universe and out. It fails on those grounds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried to be fair and consider this a separate article from the original, since I downvoted that article for different reasons. Hence this article gets its own downvote for the crime of being not funny to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see the punchline, but since I don't see how it's funny, I'm downvoting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh! Good point! Upvoted as part of a nifty scip, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep in mind that it has the -J title, but it is tagged as a supplement; it has the title, but otherwise isn't considered (or presumably shouldn't be considered) a regular -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't quite downvote this, because I think the conceit is clever, but this article in itself has a fairly serious problem as a -J: it's not funny. Come up with a joke entry about fairies that's actually amusing on its own (beyond "Haha, fairies, how silly! And my word, they don't talk like proper little Victorian ladies at all!"), and I'll enthusiastically vote it upward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think it's supposed to be funny. It's a joke, but the punchline is in the very, very last line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid I'm going to have to apply my usual -J evaluation criterion to this: I don't think it's funny. It acts like it's subverting tropes for humor, but it does so at a pretty shallow level and doesn't really take that anywhere funny, unless you think yelling and cursing is inherently amusing.

Maybe it's meant to parody things I'm not familiar with? Either way, it doesn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm… this is weird. Not the article itself inasmuch as the implication that -Js are canon as fictional accounts. Which for the most part makes sens. But on the other hand, I quite enjoy thinking about the universes where -Js are real… but I feel that we'd be getting pretty Borgean if we go down that rabbithole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, 100% organic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was this born out of Bright challenge?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very creative use of the -J type.

item.title: Author Commentary
item.content:encoded: Thanks to EldritchCyanide, Davsto, and Dr Leonard for reviewing this.
